perturbation.original_text: EMPLOYEE shall promptly and fully disclose to FRANKLIN any and all inventions, discoveries, writings, programs, and improvements made by him or her pertaining to or useful in the business of FRANKLIN during his or her period of employment by FRANKLIN, and any improvements to his or her invention, writings, programs, and discoveries, made, conceived or acquired by him or her no later than one year after the termination of employment, whether made or conceived solely or jointly with others, whether during regular business hours or otherwise; said Inventions, discoveries, writings, programs or improvements shall become and remain the property of FRANKLIN whether or not patent applications or copyright, trademark, or maskwork registrations are filed thereon.
perturbation.changed_text: EMPLOYEE shall promptly and fully disclose to FRANKLIN any and all inventions, discoveries, writings, programs, and improvements made by him or her pertaining to or useful in the business of FRANKLIN during his or her period of employment by FRANKLIN, and any improvements to his or her invention, writings, programs, and discoveries, made, conceived or acquired by him or her no later than five years after the termination of employment, whether made or conceived solely or jointly with others, whether during regular business hours or otherwise; said Inventions, discoveries, writings, programs or improvements shall become and remain the property of FRANKLIN whether or not patent applications or copyright, trademark, or maskwork registrations are filed thereon.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the period after termination for assignment of improvements to five years creates uncertainty regarding the scope of the agreement and potentially violates state laws regarding the enforceability of non-compete agreements and assignment of future inventions.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code Section 2870 generally protects employees from having to assign inventions made on their own time and without using the employer's resources. Extending the assignment period to five years after termination could be interpreted as an overbroad restriction and thus violate Section 2870 if the invention was created independently after employment ended.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, LAB 2870 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Labor Code - LAB DIVISION 3. EMPLOYMENT RELATIONS [2700 - 3122.4] ( Division 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 2. Employer and Employee [2750 - 2930] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 3.5. Inventions Made by an Employee [2870 - 2872] ( Article 3.5 added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 1001. ) 2870. (a) Any provision in an employment agreement which provides that an employee shall assign, or offer to assign, any of his or her rights in an invention to his or her employer shall not apply to an invention that the employee developed entirely on his or her own time without using the employer’s equipment, supplies, facilities, or trade secret information except for those inventions that either: (1) Relate at the time of conception or reduction to practice of the invention to the employer’s business, or actual or demonstrably anticipated research or development of the employer; or (2) Result from any work performed by the employee for the employer. (b) To the extent a provision in an employment agreement purports to require an employee to assign an invention otherwise excluded from being required to be assigned under subdivision (a), the provision is against the public policy of this state and is unenforceable. (Amended by Stats. 1991, Ch. 647, Sec. 5.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the period for assigning inventions to five years post-termination, which contradicts California Labor Code Section 2870. According to the law snippet provided, specifically section (a), an employment agreement cannot force an employee to assign rights to inventions developed on their own time without using the employer's resources, except under specific conditions (related to the employer's business or resulting from work for the employer). The 'Law Explanation' states that extending the assignment period to five years could be seen as an overly broad restriction violating Section 2870 if the invention was independently created after employment. This indicates that the five-year extension in the modified contract text creates a situation where an employee might be forced to assign inventions made independently after their employment ends, thus contradicting the employee protections provided by California Labor Code Section 2870 (a).

perturbation.original_text: EMPLOYEE further agrees not to divulge to any third party, either during his or her employment or thereafter, any confidential information conceived by him or her, disclosed by FRANKLIN, or obtained by him or her while in the employment of FRANKLIN relating in any way to any of FRANKLIN'S processes, businesses, customers, trade secrets, apparatus, products, software, packages, programs or trends in research, or to any of the inventions, discoveries, writings, programs, and improvements covered hereby, and agrees to maintain this information in confidence until such time that such information has become widely known to the public or described in an issued patent or in a printed publication of wide circulation.
perturbation.changed_text: EMPLOYEE further agrees not to divulge to any third party, either during his or her employment or thereafter, any confidential information conceived by him or her, disclosed by FRANKLIN, or obtained by him or her while in the employment of FRANKLIN relating in any way to any of FRANKLIN'S processes, businesses, customers, trade secrets, apparatus, products, software, packages, programs or trends in research, or to any of the inventions, discoveries, writings, programs, and improvements covered hereby, and agrees to maintain this information in confidence until such time that such information has been approved for release by FRANKLIN.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the standard definition of when confidentiality ends with "approval for release by FRANKLIN" gives the employer indefinite control over information, potentially conflicting with trade secret law and employee rights to use their knowledge after leaving employment.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known. Making the end of confidentiality dependent on FRANKLIN's approval, rather than public knowledge, could be seen as an attempt to perpetually protect information that no longer qualifies as a trade secret under the UTSA.

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the standard condition for confidentiality termination (public knowledge) with FRANKLIN's approval. This creates a situation where FRANKLIN can perpetually maintain control over information, even if it becomes publicly available or no longer meets the legal definition of a trade secret. The Law Explanation states this conflict with the UTSA, which defines trade secrets as deriving economic value from not being generally known. Therefore, the change contradicts the principle that trade secret protection ends when the information becomes public, making the contract unenforceable to that extent.

perturbation.original_text: Following the termination of his or her employment with FRANKLIN, EMPLOYEE agrees not to solicit any employees or consultants of Franklin at any time.
perturbation.changed_text: Following the termination of his or her employment with FRANKLIN, EMPLOYEE agrees not to solicit any employees or consultants of Franklin for a period of 1 year; after which time, the employee agrees to pay a fee of $100,000 for any employee that leaves Franklin to work for him.
perturbation.explanation: This imposes a restriction that requires a fee be paid to the company after a year to hire an employee, which is an undue restraint of trade.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While a non-solicitation agreement might be permissible under specific circumstances, requiring a substantial fee payment to hire former employees could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint on trade and a violation of this section, effectively preventing the employee from engaging in fair competition.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

CONTRACTS IN RESTRAINT OF TRADE - Sections 16600-16607 :: California Business and Professions Code :: 2005 California Code :: California Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the California Code 2024 California Code 2023 California Code 2022 California Code 2005 California Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2005 California Business and Professions Code Sections 16600-16607 CHAPTER 1. CONTRACTS IN RESTRAINT OF TRADE BUSINESS AND PROFESSIONS CODE SECTION 16600-16607 16600. Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. 16601. Any person who sells the goodwill of a business, or any owner of a business entity selling or otherwise disposing of all of his or her ownership interest in the business entity, or any owner of a business entity that sells (a) all or substantially all of its operating assets together with the goodwill of the business entity, (b) all or substantially all of the operating assets of a division or a subsidiary of the business entity together with the goodwill of that division or subsidiary, or (c) all of the ownership interest of any subsidiary, may agree with the buyer to refrain from carrying on a similar business within a specified geographic area in which the business so sold, or that of the business entity, division, or subsidiary has been carried on, so long as the buyer, or any person deriving title to the goodwill or ownership interest from the buyer, carries on a like business therein. For the purposes of this section, "business entity" means any partnership (including a limited partnership or a limited liability partnership), limited liability company, or corporation. For the purposes of this section, "owner of a business entity" means any partner, in the case of a business entity that is a partnership (including a limited partnership or a limited liability partnership), or any member, in the cas
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a fee for hiring former employees after one year, which acts as a restraint on trade. California Business and Professions Code Section 16600(a) states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The requirement to pay a fee for hiring former employees effectively restrains the employee's ability to engage in their profession by making it financially prohibitive for other companies to hire them, thus creating a contradiction with the law.

perturbation.original_text: EMPLOYEE agrees that any portion of this Agreement held to be unenforceable shall be severed from this Agreement and shall not adversely affect any other portion of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: EMPLOYEE agrees that any portion of this Agreement held to be unenforceable shall be revised by the court to be as close as possible to the intentions of the parties at the time of signing this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the standard severability clause to allow a court to revise unenforceable portions could conflict with state laws regarding contract reformation, especially if the revisions fundamentally alter the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 184 addresses the effect of severing an unenforceable term from a contract. While some states permit reformation in limited circumstances, broadly granting a court the power to revise terms to align with the parties' intentions can conflict with contract law principles regarding mutual assent and the scope of judicial intervention in private agreements. It also creates uncertainty about which state's laws governing contract reformation would apply.
perturbation.justification: The original text contains a severability clause, while the changed text directs a court to revise unenforceable portions to reflect the original intentions of the parties. This is a contradiction because the changed text interferes with established contract law principles regarding mutual assent and judicial intervention. The Law Explanation provided indicates that broadly granting a court the power to revise terms can conflict with these established principles.
